questions.description: Directive 2016/200/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council on the… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Directive 2016/200/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions (fictional) establishes a number of new requirements for EU Member States, designed to ensure that only approved moneylenders are allowed to provide financial credit to customers. Article 9(1) of Directive 2016/200/EU specifically provides that: "[...] the competent national authorities shall refuse authorisation to commence the activity of a credit institution where the initial capital is less that EUR 6 million". As an exception to this, Article 9(4) of the Directive further provides that: "[m]ember States may grant authorisation to credit institutions where the initial capital is less than that specified in paragraph 1, subject to the following conditions: the initial capital is no Jess than EUR 1 million; and the Member State notifies the Commission of their reasons for exercising this option". Article 13 of the Directive provides that the directors of credit institutions should not be paid any bonus in a year where the institution has failed to make a profit in excess of EUR 1 million. Article 60 of Directive 2016/200/EU states that it was to be implemented by 1 August 2017. The Bank of Ballintubber was granted authorisation to operate credit, and other, facilities by the Central Bank of Ireland on 22 September 2017, despite having an initial capital investment of only EUR 3 million. The EU Commission was not notified about this authorisation. The Supervision of Credit Institutions (Amendment) Regulations 2017, transposing Directive 2016/200/EU, were not introduced until 3 December 2017. In January 2020, following a series of poor lending decisions and the award of large bonuses to the directors of the Bank of Ballintubber, which never made any profits, has now gone into liquidation. Secure Safes ltd. are owed EUR 238,900 by the Bank of Ballintubber for the supply and installation of timelock safes during 2018. An initial assessment by the liquidator has shown that there is no chance that unsecured creditors like Secure Safes ltd. will be paid what they are owed. Secure Safes ltd. have had to make two staff redundant as a result of this non-payment and the future of this limited company is also now in jeopardy. Advise the liquidator of Secure Safes ltd., by reference to relevant CJEU case-law, about whether the company could rely on the provisions of Directive 2016/200/EU in separate actions that the liquidator is considering authorising it to bring before the Irish courts against each of: (i) the State; (ii) the Central Bank of Ireland; and (iii) the directors of the Bank of Ballintubber.

questions.description: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has changed within the EU legal order since its foundation in the 1950s and how the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights has, since it became legally binding, now become the main basis on which the EU Courts ensure that human rights are observed in the Union legal order.

questions.description: In 2018, the Irish Department of Health undertook a number of (fictional)… Regulation, Company.
questions.text: In 2018, the Irish Department of Health undertook a number of (fictional) studies into the safety of DIY and home improvement substances. Two of these studies identified that the inhalation of the substance 'B-xylene212' may contribute to causing cancer. The study was not, however, definitive. It also concluded that further research into the risks associated with the use of B-xylene212 would have to carried out. B-xylene212 is used by many paint manufacturers as a thinning substance for their products. In March 2019, the Irish Minister for Health signed the (fictional) Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2019, Regulation 3(2)(k) of which prohibits the use of B-xylene212 in paints used for domestic decoration. Regulation 6(4) further provides that any of the prohibitions or restrictions set out in these regulations can be retracted, or strengthened, if new, and reliable scientific studies suggest that any of the substances covered by the 2019 Irish Regulations, including B-xylene212, are, or are not, in fact toxic or dangerous in any way. In February 2020, the International Association for Research and Investigation into Cancer (IARIC) and the European Environment Agency both published findings stating that there is currently insufficient information to determine whether or not B-xylene212 causes cancer. They both declared it a low to moderate risk substance. Other '.l,oyv to moderate' risk products used to manufacture paints, such as C-Glycol55, were not included in the Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2019, or in any other similar Irish regulations. Pforzheim Paints are a German company who have manufactured paints and paint products containing B-xylene212 since 2014. They have been exporting nearly 40% of their entire output of products to Ireland since 2015. The use of B-xylene212 in the production of paints for domestic use is permitted in Germany. However, products which contain B- xylene212 and are sold in Germany must state clearly and conspicuously on the front of the tin of paint that this substance has been used in manufacture and it may be toxic. All Pforzheim Paints containing B-xylene212 also comply with this requirement. Pforzheim Paints have had to withdraw all of their products from the Irish market after the introduction of the Decorative Paints. (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2019 into Irish law. Pforzheim Paints have lodged a complaint with the EU Commission about the Irish restrictions on the sale of products containing B-xylene212. Pforzheim Paints are considering using C-Glycol55 as a thinning substance for their products instead, but the cost of C-Glycol55 is three times as much as B-xylene212. Advise the Commission, referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout your answer.

questions.description: With reference to relevant CJEU case-law and Treaty provisions, make an assessment of whether the Treaty on the…
questions.text: With reference to relevant CJEU case-law and Treaty provisions, make an assessment of whether the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union currently provides an adequate system of legal protection for individuals who seek judicial review of '...legislative acts [and] of acts of the [EU Institutions] intended to produce legal effects vis-a-vis third parties'.

questions.description: Maria is a Dutch national, currently employed as a shift worker in a…
questions.text: Maria is a Dutch national, currently employed as a shift worker in a banking computer centre in Breda, a city in the south of The Netherlands. She resides with her husband Pierre and their two children, Lucas (aged 15) and Ruben (aged 18), in the northern suburbs of Antwerp, just over the border in northern Belgium. The family moved from Breda to Antwerp just under a year ago. Lucas is in his penultimate year of secondary school, still attending the same school in Breda as he was when Maria and her family resided there. He commutes by organised school transport from his home in Antwerp every day. Neither Maria nor her husband Pierre can bring Lucas to school every day due to the shift hours Maria has to work, which are often at night, and given that Pierre voluntarily took early retirement just under a year ago from his job as a firefighter in Brussels, Belgium. A locally administered scheme in Breda bears the cost of school transport for students in the final two years of their secondary education. However, Maria has been informed that the local council will not cover these costs for Lucas as the obligation to organise and pay for this transport relates to students residing in Breda only. Ruben finished his secondary schooling at the same school in Breda last year and now attends art college in Ghent, Belgium. He has been charged the exact same enrolment fees as all other EU nationals on his course (€1,750 per annum) but his application to the public student support scheme for a loan of €5,500 to cover accommodation costs in Ghent has been refused on the basis that these financial supports are only available to people who: (i) have been made involuntarily redundant from work in the past twelve months; or (ii) are the children of a parent currently working in Belgium; or (iii) are the children of a parent who has been made involuntarily unemployed in Belgium in the past two years. Outraged by these two discrete Dutch and Belgian decisions, Maria comes to you for advice on whether the refusal to: (i) cover the school transport costs for Lucas; and (ii) provide Ruben with a student loan, constitutes unjustifiable indirect discrimination against migrant workers, as prohibited under EU law. Advise Maria, referring to relevant CJEU case-law throughout your answer.

questions.description: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted…
questions.text: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101(1) TFEU. In addition, explain separately, by reference to relevant case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undertakings" under Article 102 TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any [THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any [THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: (i) Case C-341/05, Laval un Partneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetaref6rbundet ea (2007] ECR 1-11767; (ii) Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General (2012] EGLI 756; (iii) Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs-og Selskabsstyrelsen (1999] ECR 1- 14 59; (iv) Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLl:EU:C:2018:979; (v) Case C-44/93, Namur - Les Assurances du Credit SA v Office National du Ducroire and Belgian State [1994] ECR 1-3829; (vi) Case C-413/06, Bertelsmann AG ·and Sony Corporation of America v Impala (2008] ECR 1-4951; (vii) Cases 21-24/72, International Fruit Company NV v Produktschap voor Groentenen Fruit[1972] ECR 1219; (viii) Case T-184/95, Dorsch Consult lngenieurgesellschaft mbH.Y, Council [1998] ECR 11-667.

questions.description: The Irish Government has recently introduced two (fictional) financial… Directive.
questions.text: The Irish Government has recently introduced two (fictional) financial charges on wine, regardless of where it is produced. The first is an inspection charge, which is imposed on any wine for sale in Ireland to cover the costs of analysing samples to ensure that the grapes used in production were free of 'black rot' fungus disease at the time of production. These inspections have been ordered following the- publication of a recent study by the Organic Growers of Ireland, which has suggested that the presence of this fungus could possibly have some detrimental consequences for the health of humans that consume wine produced from infected grapes. Similar inspections have not been ordered in any other EU Member States. However, (fictional) Directive 99/123 does allow Member States to analyse samples of wine, where a health protection concern has been identified. The second charge is an additional €1.50 tax on all bottles of wine that are sold by retailers for more than €20 per bottle. This is purely a revenue-raising measure. The average retail price for a bottle of wine in Ireland is €12. There is no significant level of commercial wine production in Ireland. The European Commission has started an investigation into both the (i) the sampling charge; and (ii) the additional retail tax. Advise the Government, by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law of the Union judicature, on the compatibility of both of these charges with Article 30 TFEU and Article 110 TFEU.

questions.description: How has the status of human rights within the EU legal order changed since the foundation of the EEC in the 1950s?
questions.text: How has the status of human rights within the EU legal order changed since the foundation of the EEC in the 1950s? Refer to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, Treaty developments and EU legislative initiatives that have played a role in bringing about this level of change, throughout your answer.

ANSWER PART (A) OR PART (B). DO NOT ANSWER BOTH PARTS PART (A) Article 5 of (the fictional) Directive 2016/456/EU on the approximation of maximum permitted noise-levels at outdoor music concerts provides that: "[a]ll licence-holders operating outdoor music concerts must ensure that those working at these events are subjected to a maximum noise level of 116 decibels for a maximum continuous period of four hours." Various methods are set out in the Directive for measuring the noise level at these events. There are also a series of definitions set out, which clearly classify security staff as being amongst those who are "working at these events" and are therefore covered by the protection set out in the Directive. Article 12 of the same Directive further provides that: " [m]ember States shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 31 March 2018." Prior to the introduction of Directive2016/456/EU, maximum permitted noise levels for concerts in Ireland were set out in the (fictional) Environmental Noise Act 1994. This 1994 Act allowed for exposure to a maximum noise level of 125 decibels for a continuous period of up to six hours. Both the 2016 EU Directive, and the 1994 Act, oblige the organisers of concerts to maintain records of the noise levels recorded during these events for five years. Brian was employed on a part-time basis as a security guard by Cool Concerts Ltd. between January 2017 and October 2019. He worked at these events for an average of four days per month during this period. He was regularly exposed to noise levels which exceeded 116 decibels. At one heavy metal festival at which Brian was working, held in Limerick on 3 August 2019, the noise level was recorded as having reached 124 decibels. Brian was exposed to this high noise volume for up to five hours on that day. He has been suffering from tinnitus and ear nerve damage since this date and he is no longer able to work for Cool Concerts Ltd. as a security guard due to the almost constant ringing noise in his ears and partial loss of hearing. Directive 2016/456/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Environmental Noise (Maximum Permitted Levels at Outdoor Events) Regulations in May 2020. The 2020 Irish Regulations set out a range of fines that may be imposed, up to a maximum level of €2,000 for the most serious breaches. Brian now seeks your advice on whether he can bring a claim for compensation against either: (i) Cool Concerts Ltd.; or (ii) the State, for the non-application and/or non implementation of the 2016 Directive. Your advice should focus on whether or not, by reference to relevant EU law principles as developed in pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, Brian was protected by the provisions of Directive 2016/456/EU on 3 August 2019 and, if so, whether any remedies may be available to him under EU law against Cool Concerts Ltd and/or the State. [OR]{.underline} PART (B) The CJEU in Case C-603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Union law when it stated, in paragraph 23 of the judgment, that: "[...] in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State, in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order." Have the above-referenced principles of 'equivalence' and 'practical possibility' been developed in relevant case-law by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law?

questions.description: has been supplying organic food shops all over Ireland with self-service… Company.
questions.text: Naas Natural Foods Ltd. has been supplying organic food shops all over Ireland with self-service 'zero-waste' food dispensers and products since 2016. The most popular foodstuffs that it supplies to these shops include toasted hazelnuts, dried prunes and dried figs. The idea behind its business is that customers can go to organic food shops with their own glass jars and fill these from the food dispensers, in order to cut down on single-use packaging waste. It has been very successful in this regard and currently has a turnover of more than €3 million per annum, employing 26 full-time staff. Due to the Covid-19 virus pandemic, the Irish Government on the advice of the National Pandemic Prevention Team (NPPT) (fictional), has temporarily prohibited the use of the type of food dispensers used by Naas Natural Foods ltd. as a measure designed to help curtail the spread of the virus. The NPPT claims that these dispensers could add to the spread of this virus as many people might touch the same dispenser with unsanitised hands. The Irish Government has stated that the temporary ban on the food dispensers will last at least until May 2021. Faros Figs, a Greek company, has been supplying all the figs that it produces each year since 2017 to Naas Natural Foods Ltd. Naas Natural Foods Ltd. accepts that it could switch from the supply of dispensed food to pre-packaged food, but it insists that this would go against the very reason that they set up in the first place - to offer an alternative, environmentally-friendly way of shopping for items usually sold in single-use packaging. Faros Figs and Naas Natural Foods Ltd. have been talking to each other about their predicament and believe that the decision of the Irish Government effectively to suspend their way of doing business may be contrary to some aspect of EU law, possibly Article 34 TFEU regarding measures equivalent to quantitative restrictions on imports. Naas Natural Foods Ltd. insists that there is no evidence in existence to show that its dispensers have been responsible for the spread of the virus and points that it has been regularly and meticulously sanitising the dispensers. Referring throughout your answer to relevant CJEU case-law on the application of Articles 34-36 TFEU, advise Faros Figs and Naas Natural Foods Ltd. as to whether the Irish Government's measure may be incompatible with EU law on the free movement of goods.

questions.description: Rita is a 28-year old Belgian national who moved to Ireland in March 2017.
questions.text: Rita is a 28-year old Belgian national who moved to Ireland in March 2017. She had graduated from Ghent University with an Honours Degree in English and History in 2014. She worked as a kitchen porter in Killarney for 4 months from May 2017 onwards, leaving that job to work as a classroom assistant from September 2017 for nine months in a secondary school in Kildare. Rita really enjoyed her job as a classroom assistant and decided that she would study and train to become an English and History teacher. Rita enrolled on a teacher-training correspondence course, run by a private college in Dublin, from September 2018. It is a two-year academic qualification, after which students who have passed all of their exams are placed in teaching-practice positions in schools for a full year. After the successful completion of this practical element of the education and training, students graduate as recognised qualified secondary school teachers in Ireland. However, Rita has now encountered two problems. First, she has twice presented for her Irish language exam, which is a compulsory part of her training course, but she has failed this exam on both occasions. As a result, the private teacher-education college is not allowing her to proceed to the practical part of her training. She has excelled in all other parts of her course to date - it is only the Irish language exam that she has failed to pass. Second, while she waits for the outcome of an appeal of the decision to refuse to grant her access to the practical teacher training, Rita has applied for a job as a security guard at the Department of Foreign Affairs in Dublin. Rita, who speaks fluent French, Flemish (Dutch), German and English, and is an amateur Mixed Martial Arts fanatic, thought that she would be an ideal candidate for this temporary position. After what she thought was a very successful interview, Rita has now been informed that she will not be offered the job for two reasons - 1. Because she is not an Irish national - the Department of Foreign Affairs did not realise that she was Belgian until she was interviewed; and - 2. She has a criminal record in Belgium, as she was found guilty of possessing a small quantity of cannabis with intent to supply while she was a student at Ghent University. She was sentenced to 40 hours of community service for this in 2012. The Department of Foreign Affairs have passed this information on to the Department of Justice, which has now started proceedings to deport Rita from the State as it deems that her conviction for a drugs offence renders her continued presence in Ireland contrary to public policy. Advise Rita on whether she may have any grounds to challenge: - (i) the decision of the teacher-training college to refuse to grant her access to the practical part of her education and training; (ii) the reasons for the refusal of the Department of Foreign Affairs to employ her as a security guard; and (iii) the proceedings that have been initiated by the Department of Justice to deport Rita from Ireland. Refer to relevant EU Treaty provisions and legislation, as interpreted in relevant case-law of the CJEU, throughout in support of your advice.

questions.description: When Advocate General Jacobs stated, in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on…
questions.text: When Advocate General Jacobs stated, in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 50/2000, UPA v Council that: "[ ...] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by a Community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, a substantial adverse effect on his interests" he was clearly articulating that there was a lack of effective judicial protection for non privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union's institutions. Explain briefly the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing here. In addition make an assessment of whether these concerns have been adequately addressed by the changes that were made to the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by the Treaty of Lisbon and related case-law particularly that interpreting the said new fourth paragraph of what became and was renumbered as Article 263 TFEU (upon the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon on 1 December 2009).

questions.description: Outline by reference to both relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature the…
questions.text: Outline by reference to both relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature the respective roles of each of: (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) the Council, in establishing new or amending EU legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures by the amendments introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon have improved, with effect from 1 December 2009, the democratic legitimacy of decision-making within the European Union.

questions.description: Write a case-note on [ANY THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-…
questions.text: Write a case-note on [ANY THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: (i) Cases 56 & 58/64, Etablissements Consten SARL and Grundig- Verkaufs-GmbH v Commission [1966] ECR 299; (ii) Cases 6 & 7/73, Istituto Chemioterapico Italiano SpA and Commercial Solvents v Commission [1974] ECR 223; (iii) Case C-372/04, Watts v Bedford Primary Care Trust [2006] ECR 1- 4325; (iv) Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012) ECLI 756; (v) Case 340/89, Vlassopoulou v Ministerium für Justiz, Bundes und Europaangelegenreiten Baden-Württemberg [1991] ECR 2357; (vi) Case C-236/2009, Association Beige des Consommateurs Test- Achats and others v Council [2011) ECR 1- 773; (vii) Case 87/75, Bresciani v Amministrazione ltaliana de/le Finanze [1976] ECR 129;. (viii) Case 193/85, Cooperative Co-Frutta Sri v Amministrazione de/le Finanze de/lo Stato [1987] ECR 2085.

questions.description: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear…
questions.text: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear that, in its opinion: "[...] merger control at the EU level was strengthened by the 2004 Merger Regulation reforms, particularly the introduction of the SIEC test". The Commission also opines that: "[m]ore immediately, however, there are two main ways to improve the Merger Regulation through more limited amendments. First, the Commission considers bringing acquisitions of non-controlling minority shareholdings into the scope of EU merger control. Second, there is room to further streamline case referrals in the light of the Commission's experience with the 2004 reform". How will this review of merger policy affect the way in which EU law in this area has developed since the introduction of the original Mergers Regulation in 1989 (Regulation 4064/89) and subsequent amendments, including Regulation 139/2004?
